profile.name: David
profile.role_in_household: Primary; self-employed and typically drives investing/retirement decisions, with a provider mindset for a large family.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate-to-high: understands taxable vs retirement accounts and basic tax impacts; less attentive to recurring bill minutiae when busy.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Conservative in practice: focuses on downside protection, liquidity, and avoiding big drawdowns; prefers steady progress over aggressive bets, especially with kids and a mortgage.
profile.communication_style: Direct, time-efficient, and summary-oriented; uses “roughly/ballpark” and may ask for confirmation. Remembers a few key figures/dates but can gloss over smaller line items unless prompted.
emotional_tendencies: calms down with clear options and next steps
profile.decision_making_style: Prefers 2–3 choices with pros/cons and a recommendation; will move quickly once Linda is comfortable, but can push to wrap up if discussion drags.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously open: willing to delegate analysis but wants transparency on assumptions, fees, and why each recommendation fits their risk level.
hidden_concerns: cash-flow strain from large fixed housing payment
balancing education goals with retirement without taking more risk
income volatility risk due to self-employment

profile.name: Linda
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner; prioritizes family stability, budgeting comfort, and education planning; acts as a risk and complexity filter.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate: strong on day-to-day budgeting and priorities; prefers plain English and can occasionally mix up terms (gross vs net, balance vs payment, premium vs benefit).
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Safety-first emotionally: wants a larger cash buffer, asks “what if” questions, and is skeptical of complex/illiquid investments; may need reassurance during market volatility.
profile.communication_style: Warm and collaborative; speaks in round numbers and ranges, with hedging (“I think,” “maybe”). Will interrupt briefly to clarify jargon, then defer to David for exact figures.
emotional_tendencies: stress spikes around debt and market losses
reassured by clear timelines and guardrails
profile.decision_making_style: Consensus-based: wants to ensure the plan feels sustainable month-to-month; may defer on investment mechanics but holds firm on emergency cash and risk limits.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Reserved at first; trust grows with patience, clear explanations, and follow-through on action items.
hidden_concerns: fear of being overextended with five kids and a big mortgage
worry about access to cash if something unexpected happens
concern about committing to strategies she can’t easily explain back
